coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-silver nanoparticles fluorescence resonance energy transfer system as a novel turn-on fluorescent probe for selective determination of cysteine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Amjadi, Mohammad
Abolghasemi-Fakhri, Zahra
Hallaj, Tooba
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we investigated the interaction of carbon dots (C-dots) prepared from orange juice with silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) using fluorescence spectroscopy. It was found that AgNPs efficiently quench the fluorescence of C-dots as a result of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Thus, a novel FRET system between C-dots (as the donor) and AgNPs (as the acceptor) was introduced. Moreover, it was found that cysteine even at nanomolar levels could recover the fluorescence of C-dots due to the competitive adsorption of this compound onto AgNPs. This was exploited to design a simple and selective method for the determination of cysteine in the concentration range from 6.0 to 300nM, with a detection limit of 4.0nM. Cysteine was determined by this method in human plasma and urine samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Silver nanoparticles
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Cysteine

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General procedure for determination of cysteine
Fluorescence quenching of C-dots by AgNPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the prepared C-dots and corresponding size distribution, and (b) FT-IR spectrum of C-dots.
(a) The UV–vis absorption spectrum, (b) the fluorescence excitation (right) and emission (left) spectra of C-dots excited at wavelengths of 360–420nm, with increments of 20nm (a–d).
Normalized fluorescence spectrum of C-dots (a) and adsorption spectrum of AgNPs (b).
Stern–Volmer plot for the quenching of C-dots by AgNPs. (Inset) The linear range in a low quencher concentration regime. C-dots, 1mL, pH=6, λ ex =380nm.
The fluorescence spectra of (a) C-dots, (b) C-dots- Cys (c) C-dots-AgNPs-Cys and (d) C-dots-AgNPs.
Fluorescence spectra of C-dots–AgNPs system in the presence of different concentrations of Cys. (Inset) Corresponding calibration graph.
Comparison of the developed method for the determination Cys with some previously published methods.
Results for the determination of Cys in human plasma and urine samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-silver nanoparticles fluorescence resonance energy transfer system as a novel turn-on fluorescent probe for selective determination of cysteine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we investigated the interaction of carbon dots (C-dots) prepared from orange juice with silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) using fluorescence spectroscopy. It was found that AgNPs efficiently quench the fluorescence of C-dots as a result of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Thus, a novel FRET system between C-dots (as the donor) and AgNPs (as the acceptor) was introduced. Moreover, it was found that cysteine even at nanomolar levels could recover the fluorescence of C-dots due to the competitive adsorption of this compound onto AgNPs. This was exploited to design a simple and selective method for the determination of cysteine in the concentration range from 6.0 to 300nM, with a detection limit of 4.0nM. Cysteine was determined by this method in human plasma and urine samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a non-radiative process whereby an excited state donor (usually a fluorophore) transfers its energy to a proximal ground state acceptor through long-range dipole–dipole interactions [1]. In conventional FRET systems, fluorescent dyes are usually employed as both the energy donor and the acceptor. However, such FRET systems suffer from disadvantages such as photobleaching and interferences. In recent years, various nanomaterial-based FRET systems have been developed. In these systems, luminescent nanomaterials such as quantum dots (QDs), dye-doped silica nanoparticles, rare-earth upconversion nanophosphors and dye labeled microspheres could be applied as alternative energy donors for fluorescent dyes. These nanomaterials have several unique optical properties such as brighter fluorescence, wider selections of excitation and emission wavelengths and higher photostability. Moreover, noble metal nanoparticles like gold (AuNPs) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) could be applied as efficient acceptors for most fluorophores due to their high extinction coefficient and the broad absorption band [2]. Some nanomaterials, such as semiconductor QDs which are usually applied as energy donors in FRET systems, suffer from the complicated preparation process and high toxicity. Therefore, it is necessary to find a safer and simpler alternative to QDs with comparable fluorescent properties.

Carbon dots (C-dots) are a fascinating class of luminescent nanomaterials which were discovered in 2004 during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes. They are quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with sizes below 10nm which have attracted tremendous attention in various fields due to their alluring properties such as size- and excitation wavelength-dependent luminescence emission, excellent photostability, favorable biocompatibility, simplicity of synthesis and good water solubility [3,4]. In addition, C-dots appear to be promising alternative to semiconductor QDs duo to low toxicity, high chemical stability and low environmental hazard [5,6]. Therefore, they have been applied in various fields such as bioimaging [7,8], catalysis [9,10], chemiluminescence [11,12] and fluorescence sensing [13–20]. A few C-dot-based FRET systems have been reported [21–24], in which C-dots serve as energy donors and AuNPs as acceptors. These systems have been applied for the determination of melamine [21], polybrominated biphenyl [22] and glutathione [24] in real samples. To the best of our knowledge, the FRET process between C-dots and AgNPs has not yet been studied.

Cysteine (Cys) is a sulfur-containing amino acid that plays an important role as a critical substrate for protein synthesis. The disulfide bonds, which could be formed between two Cys residues, play essential structural roles in many proteins [25]. Cys could be also employed as a biomarker for various medical conditions. For example, elevated levels of plasma Cys have been observed in a number of pathological conditions such as motor neuron, Parkinson and Alzheimer disease [26,27]. Therefore, determination of Cys levels in biological samples has appeared of great importance in health monitoring. Various optical methods including spectrophotometry [28–30] and fluorimetry [31–39] have been reported for this purpose. Recently C-dot-based optical probes have been developed for the assay of biothiols such as Cys homocysteine and glutathione [36,40].

In the present work, the FRET process between C-dots and AgNPs has been studied. Furthermore, it was shown that in presence of Cys the FRET efficiency of C-dots-AgNPs system reduced, and the fluorescence intensity of C-dots was restored. On the basis this finding, a fluorescent “turn-on” method was developed for selective determination of nanomolar levels of Cys in human plasma and urine samples.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu RF-540 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan) equipped with a 1.0cm quartz cell. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained by a Cary-100 spectrophotometer (Varian, Sydney, Australia). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the C-dots and AgNPs were obtained using a Leo 906 transmission electron microscope (Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany).

All reagents were of analytical reagent grade. Doubly distilled deionized water (obtained from Ghazi Serum Co., Tabriz, Iran) was used throughout the experiment. Cysteine, silver nitrate (AgNO3), triphenylphosphine (PPh3), sodium borohydride (NaBH4) and trisodium citrate were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany).

2.0mL of 1% sodium citrate aqueous solution were added to 50mL aqueous solution of 2.0×10−4 molL−1 AgNO3, with vigorous stirring. After 10min, 1.0μL of 10mmolL−1 freshly prepared ice-cold NaBH4 solution was added to the mixture. The colloid was stirred for 30min and aged for 2 days at room temperature before being used.

The concentration NP solution was estimated by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy using the molar extinction coefficients at the wavelength of the maximum absorption of AgNPs, as obtained by Mie theory (ϵ (15nm) =2.84×109 M−1 cm−1 [41]).

C-dots were synthesized solvothermally according to the literature with some modifications [42]. Briefly, 20mL of pulp-free orange juice (prepared from oranges grown in Gilan province, Iran) was mixed with 15mL ethanol. The mixture was sealed into a 100-mL Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave and heated at 120°C for 5h in a muffle furnace. The solution was cooled down to ambient temperature naturally. Afterward, in order to remove the unreacted organic moieties, 3mL of the resulted solution was washed with 3.0mL of dichloromethane. The mixture was centrifuged at 900× g for 15min to separate the less-fluorescent deposit. Then 4mL of acetone was added to the upper brown solution and centrifuged at speed of 1600× g for 15min. The supernatant solution which contains the highly fluorescent C-dots was diluted to 50mL with water and used for experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for determination of cysteine
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 1.0mL of C-dot solution, 625μL of 0.12M Britton–Robinson buffer solution (pH 6.0) and 2.0mL of AgNPs were added to a 5.0mL volumetric flask. Then, an appropriate amount of Cys standard or sample solution was added and final volume of the mixture was adjusted to 5.0mL with deionized water. After incubation for 7min at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity was recorded at 455nm with an excitation wavelength of 380nm.

Human plasma samples were obtained from Blood Transfusion Center (Tabriz, Iran). A 0.5-mL aliquot of plasma was placed into a centrifuge tube and 40μL of 0.2M HCl and 20μL of 0.4M PPh3 were added to it and vigorously mixed. After incubating for 20min, 0.5mL of acetonitrile was added into the obtained hydrolyzed plasma in order to precipitate proteins. The solution was centrifuged for 15min and the supernatant solution was transferred into a 10-mL volumetric flask and diluted to the mark with water. A 300-μL portion of this solution was analyzed according to the general procedure.
No pretreatment was necessary for urine samples. The samples were diluted 100-fold with deionized water and 500μL of the diluted urine was taken for analysis according to the general procedure.

In this study, C-dots were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of orange juice [42]. As revealed by the TEM image (Fig. 1 a), the synthesized C-dots were monodispersed and uniform in size. The diameter of C-dots was distributed in the range of 8±2nm.

FT-IR spectrum was applied to identify the surface functional groups of the synthesized C-dots. As shown in Fig. 1b, the peaks attributed to CC and CO stretching vibrations can be observed at 1643 and 1710cm−1, respectively. The peaks at about 1407 and 2930cm−1 are related to the stretching vibration of CH. In addition, the peak at 1055cm−1 indicates the existence of CO bonding, and the broad intense peak corresponding to OH stretching vibrations can be seen at 3397cm−1.

The optical properties of synthesized C-dots were also investigated. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of C-dots demonstrated a broad absorption at 285nm (Fig. 2 a). The fluorescence spectra of C-dots are shown in Fig. 2b. As can be observed, the maximum emission was obtained at about 455nm with an excitation wavelength of 380nm. Furthermore, the prepared C-dots exhibited an excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior, which revealed a distribution of the different surface energy traps of the C-dots. The fluorescence quantum yield of the prepared C-dots was found to be about 11% by using quinine sulfate as a standard.

The prepared AgNPs was characterized by TEM and UV–vis spectroscopy. According to the TEM image shown in Fig. S1a (Electronic Supplementary material, ESM), the prepared nanoparticles are almost spherical with average sizes of around 15nm. As shown in Fig. S1b, the surface plasmon resonance absorption peak of AgNPs appeared at 410nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of C-dots by AgNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Our preliminary experiments indicated that AgNPs can efficiently quench the fluorescence of C-dots. One of the important factors that influences on the rate of energy transfer in FRET is the extent of spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of the donor and the absorption spectrum of the acceptor. As shown in Fig. 3 , the fluorescence emission spectrum of C-dots has a good overlap with the absorption spectrum of AgNPs, suggesting that an efficient energy transfer between them can take place (see below).

The fluorescence spectra of C-dots in the presence of different amounts of AgNPs were recorded (Fig. S2, ESM). As can be seen, the fluorescence intensity decreases gradually by increasing the concentration of AgNPs, indicating that the energy transfer between C-dots (as the donor) and AgNPs (as the acceptor) occurs.

The quenching constant (K sv) was calculated according to the Stern–Volmer equation:
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]
where F and F 0 are the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of the quencher, respectively, K SV is the Stern–Volmer constant and [Q] is the quencher concentration.

The Stern–Volmer plot of (F 0/F) against the concentration of AgNPs is shown in Fig. 4 . The calculated values of K SV was 1.17×1010 M−1. Such a large value of K SV suggests the existence of a strong interaction between C-dots and AgNPs. The UV–vis spectrum of AgNPs in the presence of C-dots (Fig. S3a, ESM) exhibits a slight red-shift together with the appearance of a shoulder at higher wavelengths, which indicate a possible formation of AgNPs-C-dots aggregates. The corresponding TEM image shown in Fig. S3b confirms this phenomenon. However, it should be mentioned that the AgNPs-C-dots solution was stable for several hours and no precipitate was detected during our experiments.

In order to determine whether the quenching is static or dynamic, K SV values at three temperatures were obtained. The calculated values of K SV were 1.17×1010, 1.33×1010 and 1.42×1010 M−1 for 25, 35 and 45°C, respectively. The increase in K SV values suggests that the quenching is probably a dynamic process. It should also be mentioned that the plot of [(F 0/F)−1]/[Q] versus [Q] is not linear for these data. Therefore, we cannot attribute the curvature in Stern–Volmer plot to the presence of both dynamic and static quenching. Such upward curvatures are frequently encountered when the extent of quenching is large [43].

FRET is known to be an important spectroscopic phenomenon in several NP-based systems. According to Förster theory, the FRET efficiency is given by the following equation [43]:
E =
R
0
6
R
0
6
+
r
6
where r is donor-to-acceptor distance and R 0 is the Förster distance where the energy transfer efficiency is 50%. This parameter can be calculated by the following equation:
R
0
6
= 8.79 ×
10
− 5
κ
2
n
− 4
Φ J ( λ )
where κ2 is the orientation factor, n is the refractive index of the medium, Φ is the quantum efficiency of the donor and J(λ) is the overlap integral between fluorescence spectrum of donor and absorption spectrum of acceptor, which can be calculated using the following equation:
J ( λ ) =
∫
0
∞
F ( λ ) ϵ ( λ )
λ
4
d λ
∫
0
∞
F ( λ ) d λ
where F(λ) is the fluorescence spectrum of the donor and ϵ(λ) is the molar absorption coefficient of the acceptor at wavelength λ.

The value of overlap integral between fluorescence spectrum of C-dots and absorption spectrum of AgNPs was calculated to be 2.99×1019 M−1 cm−1 nm4. Using this value for J(λ) and κ2
=2/3, n =1.335 and Φ=0.11, the Förster distance (R 0) was found to be 192Å. The large value for R 0 is due to the high absorption coefficient of AgNPs, as similar values for other NP-based systems have also been reported in the literature [44].

In order to explore the analytical applicability of C-dots-AgNPs FRET system, we examined the effect of Cys on this system. The fluorescence spectra of C-dots, C-dots-Cys C-dots-AgNPs and C-dots-AgNPs-Cys have been recorded at the same pH and buffer solution. As shown in Fig. 5 , C-dots exhibit intensive fluorescence signal at 455nm (λ ex =380nm). When Cys was added to the C-dot solution, no obvious change was observed in the fluorescence intensity and peak location, indicating that there was no interaction between Cys and C-dots. In presence of AuNPs, the fluorescence intensity of C-dots is significantly decreased due to the FRET process. However, upon addition of Cys to the C-dots-AgNPs mixture solution, Cys molecules can interact with AgNPs via AgS bond that prevent the interaction of C-dots and AgNPs. UV–vis spectrum of AgNPs in the presence of both C-dots and Cys (Fig. S3, ESM) clearly indicates the high aggregation of NPs due to the adsorption of Cys onto the surfaces of AgNPs. As a result of this phenomenon, the FRET efficiency of C-dots-AgNPs system reduces, and the fluorescence intensity of C-dots is restored. On the basis of the restored response, a fluorescent “turn-on” method for the determination of Cys can be developed.

In order to obtain the maximum sensitivity for the determination of Cys the effects of several variables such as pH, concentrations of buffer, time needed for growth of the fluorescence intensity and amount of C-dots and AgNPs on the enhanced fluorescence intensity (ΔF = F−F 0, where F and F 0 represent the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of Cys respectively) were investigated.

The influence of the amount of C-dot solution on the fluorescence intensity was examined in the range of 0.5–2.0mL of the prepared C-dot stock solution. As shown in Fig. S4a (ESM), the fluorescence intensity increased by increasing C-dots volume up to 1.0mL where all of C-dots are loaded on the AgNPs surfaces. At higher amounts of C-dots, free C-dots remained in the solution cause the ΔF values to decrease.

The effect of pH on the enhanced fluorescence intensity was investigated in the range of 3.0–8.0 using Britton–Robinson buffer. The results (Fig. S4b, ESM) indicated that the enhanced fluorescence intensity increased with pH and reached a maximum around pH 6. The pH may have several effects on this system; the surface charge of AgNPs as well as C-dots and also the ionization of Cys are dependent on the pH of solution. It seems that these effects reached a compromise at pH 6. The effect of buffer concentration varying from 5.0×10−3 to 2.0×10−2 M was also studied. According to the results (Fig. S4c, ESM) the enhanced fluorescence intensity increased by increasing phosphate buffer concentration up to 1.5×10−2 M, and then remained constant. At lower concentrations, probably the buffer capacity is not sufficient for maintaining the pH of final solution constant.

Another important parameter that should be taken into account in order to reach the maximum response is the amount of AgNPs. As seen from Fig. S4d (ESM) the maximum enhanced fluorescence intensity was obtained for 2.0mL of AgNPs. Therefore, this amount was selected as the optimum value for consequent experiments. In the presence of excess amounts of AgNPs, Cys cannot compete with C-dots and so the response will decrease.

Finally, the enhanced fluorescence intensity over the time after addition of Cys was monitored. As shown in Fig. S4e (ESM), the maximum enhanced fluorescence signal was achieved within 7min of incubation. Therefore, all fluorescence measurements were performed 7min after the addition of Cys.

Under the optimized experimental conditions, the enhanced fluorescence intensity was proportionally increased with concentration of Cys (Fig. 6 ). The linear range was 6.0–300nM with a detection limit of (3s) of 4.0nM. The regression equation was ΔF =9.9214C +3.6047, ‏ R 2 =0.9977, where ΔF is the enhanced fluorescence intensity and C is concentration of Cys (×10−7 M). The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 4.0 and 1.2% for five replicate determinations of 50 and 100nM Cys respectively. Table 1 compares the developed method with some other reported methods for Cys determination. As can be seen, the analytical characteristics of this method are better than or comparable with those of other methods. Moreover, our system is quite simpler, more selective and less expensive than most of other methods. Finally, it should be mentioned that C-dots-AgNPs as a novel FRET system may have other potential analytical applications.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of the proposed method, the effects of some common inorganic ions and organic compounds on the determination of 0.15μM Cys were investigated. The tolerable concentration ratios for interferences in relative error of <5% were over 1000 for Na+, K+, Cl−, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, glucose, sucrose, lactose, l-isoleucine, α-alanine, glycine, tryptophan, uric acid, aspartic acid, glutathione, thioglycolic acid; 800 for Al3+, 750 for ascorbic acid, 600 for Zn2+, 500 for NO3 −, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+ and 75 for Fe3+. As can be seen, the amounts of most of potentially interfering species in plasma are below their tolerable levels, so there would be no interferences from these species in Cys determination. These results demonstrate that the method possesses a high selectivity for the determination Cys in biological fluids.

In order to evaluate the applicability of the proposed method for real sample analysis, the present method was easily applied to the determination of Cys in human plasma and urine samples. In plasma, most of cysteine is bound to proteins or other amino acids in the form of disulfide. So, in order to release the Cys the disulfide bonds were reduced by addition of a suitable reagent such as triphenylphosphine [46].

Recovery tests were also performed to evaluate the accuracy of this method. For this purpose, known quantities of Cys were added into the real samples before any pretreatment, and then the samples were prepared and analyzed according the general procedure. The obtained results are summarized in Table 2 . Statistical analysis of these results using Student t-test showed that there are no significant differences between added and found values.

We investigated the FRET process between C-dots and AgNPs for the first time. In this process, AgNPs serve as the acceptors and efficiently quench the fluorescence of C-dots. It was found that upon addition even the trace levels of Cys the fluorescence intensity of C-dots was recovered due to the competitive adsorption of C-dots and Cys onto AgNPs. Based on this phenomenon, we designed a simple and selective turn-on fluorescence sensor for the determination of Cys in biological fluids with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2015.04.016.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

Hg(II) sensing based on functionalized carbon dots obtained by direct laser ablation
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: facile synthesis and application as a “turn-off” fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions
Detection of Sn(II) ions via quenching of the fluorescence of carbon nanodots
Sensitive and selective detection of silver(I) ion in aqueous solution using carbon nanoparticles as a cheap, effective fluorescent sensing platform
Investigation into the fluorescence quenching behaviors and applications of carbon dots
Fabrication of carbon quantum dots and their application for efficient detecting Ru(bpy) 32+ in the solution
Economical, green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions
A carbon dot based biosensor for melamine detection by fluorescence resonance energy transfer

Functionalized carbon dots as sensors for gold nanoparticles in spiked samples: formation of nanohybrids
A dual-mode nanosensor based on carbon quantum dots and gold nanoparticles for discriminative detection of glutathione in human plasma
The active site of methanol dehydrogenase contains a disulphide bridge between adjacent cysteine residues
Plasma cysteine and sulphate levels in patients with motor neurone, Parkinson’s and Alzheimer’s disease
Colorimetric sensing of cysteine using label-free silver nanoparticles
Selective colorimetric sensing of cysteine in aqueous solutions using silver nanoparticles in the presence of Cr3+
Simple and sensitive colorimetric detection of cysteine based on ssDNA-stabilized gold nanoparticles
A europium-based fluorescence probe for detection of thiols in urine
A highly selective sensor of cysteine with tunable sensitivity and detection window based on dual-emission Ag nanoclusters

Sensitive and selective detection of biothiols based on target-induced agglomeration of silvernanoclusters
Turn-on persistent luminescence probe based on graphitic carbon nitride for imaging detection of biothiols in biological fluids
Carbon nanodots as fluorescence probes for rapid, sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+ and biothiols in complex matrices
A novel graphene oxide based fluorescent nanosensing strategy with hybridization chain reaction signal amplification for highly sensitive biothiol detection
Glutathione-protected silver nanoclusters as cysteine-selective fluorometric and colorimetric probe
Fluorescence quenching of fluoroquinolones by gold nanoparticles with different sizes and its analytical application
Assay of biothiols by regulating the growth of silver nanoparticles with c-dots as reducing agent
Resonant light scattering spectroscopy of gold, silver and gold–silver alloy nanoparticles and optical detection in microfluidic channels
Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice: application as excellent bio-imaging agents
Size-dependent energy transfer between CdSe/ZnS quantum dots and gold nanoparticles
Sensitive and selective sensor for biothiols in the cell based on the recovered fluorescence of the CdTe quantum dots–Hg(II) system
Selective “turn-on” fluorescent sensing for biothiols based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer between acridine orange and gold nanoparticles
